title: Does Intel's Decision Not To Manufacture InfiniBand Silicon Damage the Initiative?
author: Russ Craig, Peter S. Kastner
source_file: 2002 Does Intel_s Decision Not To Manufactur...iniBand Silicon Damage the Initiative PSK.pdf
abstract: InfiniBand, a next-generation data-center I/O initiative, faced a major challenge in August 2002 when strong backer Intel canceled plans to supply silicon for InfiniBand interface adapters ahead of the initiative's production launch. While most industry observers predicted InfiniBand would stall, Aberdeen argued the opposite: Intel's withdrawal was rational for Intel and ultimately beneficial for the InfiniBand ecosystem, enabling specialized silicon vendors to fill the gap.
importance_rationale: This InSight addressed a pivotal moment in data-center interconnect history when InfiniBand's viability was genuinely in question. Aberdeen's contrarian view — that Intel's exit would help rather than hurt the ecosystem — was highly notable and proved accurate as Mellanox rose to lead the market.
relevance_rationale: InfiniBand remains a dominant fabric in HPC and AI training clusters (now owned by NVIDIA via Mellanox acquisition in 2020). The analysis of how a major chip vendor's market exit can benefit an ecosystem is broadly applicable to current GPU/AI accelerator supply chain debates.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen's prediction that Intel's silicon exit would not damage InfiniBand and would benefit the ecosystem proved correct: Mellanox Technologies emerged as the dominant InfiniBand silicon vendor, and InfiniBand became the standard HPC/AI cluster interconnect. NVIDIA acquired Mellanox for $7B in 2020.
